mappings.rationaleAI: The Bhagavad Gita as a key Hindu text covers dharma, atman, karma yoga, and the three paths of duties, knowledge, and devotion — directly relevant to post-Vedic Sanskrit literature (B.1.b), Hindu philosophy (B.3), and the three margas (C.1.c.iii).

The Ramayana as one of the great Sanskrit epics covers Hindu mythology, dharma, and devotion with enormous cultural influence — relevant to post-Vedic literature (B.1.b), Hindu mythology (B.2), and religious expression in the arts (C.4.b).

The Upanishads as foundational Hindu metaphysical texts covering Brahman, Atman, karma, samsara, and moksha directly address the religion of the Upanishads (A.3.c), primary scriptures (B.1.a), Hindu philosophy (B.3), and views about God (C.1.a).
Lakshmi as consort of Vishnu with worship during Diwali directly addresses Vaishnavism (C.2.b) and icons expressing theological elements of Hinduism (C.4.a.ii).

The Kama Sutra's treatment of dharma, artha, and kama as life goals addresses the Hindu conception of human paths and duties (C.1.c.iii), while its discussion of social conduct relates to Hindu rituals and social practices (C.3.a).
Angkor Wat as a Hindu temple dedicated to Vishnu with bas-reliefs depicting Hindu epics exemplifies the religious expression of Hindu values in art and architecture across Southeast Asia (C.4.b).
Zoroastrianism as the religion of the Parsi community in India directly addresses Parsiism: Zoroastrianism in India (D.3).
Covers Hindu nationalism as one variety of religious fundamentalism — relevant to ethical, social, and nationalist movements in modern Hinduism (C.2.f).
The Buddha's life and teachings including the relationship between Buddhism and Hinduism addresses the heterodox period's challenges to Brahmanism (A.4).

Al-Biruni's comparative study of Indian civilization in his Kitab al-Hind provides an external scholarly perspective on Hindu beliefs and practices, relevant to the Hindu period of interaction with Islamic civilization (A.8).
The Silk Road's spread of religions including Buddhism through the Kushan network is relevant to the early Hindu period's cultural exchanges (A.5).
